data.title: Not Every Dip Is An Opportunity - Oracle Remains The Weakest Link
data.description:
Oracle’s 30% share price drop in the last 2 months was reason enough for some investors to turn bullish on the stock. Nevertheless, at $225, Oracle still trades at levels only just reached 4 months ago. What looks like a dip is not one in the longer time frame. The drop only erased the irrational gains after the announcement of the almost 4x increase in Remaining Performance Obligations [RPO] during Q1, mainly consisting of OpenAI commitments. With that, there is no reason to become euphoric just for the sake of a drop having occurred.

Why The Surge Was Irrational

The impressive-sounding RPO lump sum basket of $455 billion alone does not necessarily offer insight into the timing of future revenues, but Oracle has laid out a timeline how Cloud Infrastructure revenues will likely develop for each of the upcoming 5 fiscal years. Based on that, refer to the chart below to see how analysts have processed all this information into upward revised total revenue forecasts. The 4-year revenue CAGR prior to the announcement was 20% from 2026 to 2030, whereas it now is 35%. In aggregate, analysts thus have added a total of almost $200 billion to their prior revenue forecasts over the upcoming five fiscal years. The rest is either expected to turn into revenue after that time frame or is viewed as unlikely to be realized.

Analysts appear to be relatively in agreement regarding this new revenue consensus, as the variation coefficient of 14 to 42 analysts over the next five years is mostly below 10%. However, we must also acknowledge that the consensus is somewhat skewed towards the upper end of the range. These two charts and their discussion were just to set the basis for my next points of discussion.

The almost $200 billion in expected aggregate revenue addition for the next five years roughly corresponds to an almost 40% increase compared to the aggregate assumption prior to the announcement. The stock climbed exactly those almost 40% on the day of the report. However, this surge was irrational in three ways.

First, revenue alone says nothing about value creation, as it might come with lower margins or even losses.

Second, the 40% addition of aggregate revenue over several years implies that a large part of the increase will only be realized in 5 or more years, for example. This must be discounted due to the time value of money. A 40% increase over multiple years is not worth 40% more today.

Third, potential additional operating income will not only be generated for shareholders, but also for debt investors. This brings us to the next chapter.

Debt Investors Versus Equity Investors – Who Is Right?

Investors soon realized that. While the stock erased its post-announcement gains, Oracle bonds sold off as well. Lots of recent headlines have talked about surging CDS prices, while the same is effectively visible in Oracle bond yields, which might be easier to grasp for some. The annualized yield-to-maturity of a long-term Oracle bond maturing in 2055 has increased by 11%, or 0.6 percentage points to well over 6% over the last two months. Debt investors are demanding significantly higher returns to take on the risk of carrying Oracle bonds. This will become increasingly relevant for future bond issuances.

The stock’s earnings yield, which is the reciprocal of the P/E ratio, also climbed back to 2%, equating to a still-high TTM GAAP P/E of 50x.

Next, look at the differential between the Oracle bond yield (6.2%) to the stock’s earnings yield (2%) of currently 4.2%. Notably, this difference has widened from only 3.8% back in summer. Be aware that this comparison comes with extreme limitations such as ignoring that an earnings yield factors in growth dynamics. On the other hand, earnings yields should price in an equity risk premium, which a bond yield does not include. Despite that, bond investors now require even higher compensation for holding debt securities than equity investors for holding the stock, compared to only a few months ago (4.2% differential versus 3.8% differential).

This development appears counter intuitive. Either bond investors are now too cautious, and yields will come down again, or the stock has to drop further to match the markets overall risk perception of Oracle.

What you see below is how Oracle went from carrying a solid net cash position to ever-increasing debt and eroding cash. But absolute numbers alone might be unfair, so please look at the second chart below, which shows debt metrics relative to earnings power. Here, net debt to EBITDA has long reached the critical level of around 4x, while interest coverage remains justifiable at around 5x. Nevertheless, these are reported numbers, which do not yet account for the further issuance of debt required going forward.

Negative Free Cash Flow For Years To Come

Further debt will likely be needed to build capacity to fulfill RPOs. The company’s cash flow is far from sufficient to cover these expenditures and free cash flow has already turned negative. Free cash flow margin hovered around 33% during the company’s peak years and is currently -10% on a TTM basis. With that, we see the root cause for the necessary debt issuances visualized below.

Further deterioration of FCF is expected in the upcoming years, also rendering Oracle’s dividend unjustifiable during that period. If continued, it would essentially be debt-funded, alongside capex.

Adjusted EPS is pointing upwards regardless of these investments. Here, again, it must be questioned whether these EPS figures show a reliable picture of reality. As I have pointed out for multiple other hyperscalers in prior articles, accounting-based income figures such as EPS partly rely on assumptions about the useful life of assets. Contrary to what accelerating development-cycles would suggest, hyperscalers have increased useful lives of assets in recent years, which helps to lower annual depreciation expenses and to increase net income.

Again, I have expressed the same criticism in past coverage of Microsoft or Meta, but at least some of them are still significantly cash flow positive, which mitigates some accounting creativity.

Below you see how implied depreciation years have consistently increased far above historical averages at Oracle in recent years. I calculate this metric at a very high level by dividing gross PP&E by depreciation. The absolute number is thus very rough, as I do not differentiate between some arguably non-depreciable assets in the basket, but the trend is valid and telling. To conclude, we can question EPS projections and be cautious not to attach too much importance to forward P/E in this case.

Oracle Is Now Solely Driven By Cloud Infrastructure

This article almost solely focused on Oracle’s Cloud Infrastructure business, as this is the only segment that is currently driving its value. The company’s legacy businesses Software, Hardware and Services, accounting for roughly half of the company’s revenue, are all largely stagnant. Cloud revenues, on the other hand, recently grew between 25% and 30%, driven by infrastructure growth of over 50%. Oracle announced that cloud infrastructure would contribute $144 billion in revenue by 2030, which would equate to two thirds of total company revenue based on current consensus of $224 billion.

As such, Oracle is essentially a major AI infrastructure play, in which other areas are now overshadowed. With more than half of the almost half a trillion dollars in RPOs coming from OpenAI alone, Oracle’s infrastructure AI play is furthermore exceptionally concentrated and risky. After all, the question of how OpenAI will finance its over a trillion dollars in commitments to the whole industry is still unanswered and necessitates huge future success for OpenAI. Contrast that with OpenAI facing increasingly relevant competition, for example from Google’s progress in Gemini.

Valuation

Due to Oracle’s negative free cash flow, we cannot observe a meaningful FCF multiple or FCF yield for the company. Let us assume the historically high FCF margin of 30% to be reached again by 2030, which is extraordinarily optimistic. Applied to the 2030 revenue consensus of $224 billion, this would result in an FCF of $67 billion, which would result in an 11x 2030 FWD EV / FCF multiple based on today’s EV of $730 billion. This might sound low, but it is 5 years in the future and hinges on a highly optimistic FCF margin assumption by then.

Regarding adjusted P/E, the current multiple of 37x is above its 3-year average of 25x and its 5-year average of 22x. By the end of FY 2028, the multiple would fall to 22x based on today’s prices and consensus for adjusted EPS, which again hinges on optimistic depreciation schedules. So, valuation offers no compelling argument either.

Takeaway

Summing it all up, any potential near-term rally in the tech sector might be an opportunity to sell Oracle rather than a sign of a comeback. Oracle is at the losing end of the spectrum with its high reliance on RPOs from OpenAI alone, which is questionable in and of itself. As long as the music is playing, sellers of infrastructure, like Nvidia, will be the winners. Within the realm of infrastructure buyers, however, other hyperscalers like Microsoft and Alphabet are significantly better positioned in terms of internal financing ability through free cash flow. If the narrative starts to wiggle, Oracle is probably one of the weakest links.

data.title: Too Big And Frail? Nvidia Q3 FY26 Earnings Review
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for U.S. recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice and nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Numbers Go Up.. Until They Don’t

OK, let’s deal first of all with the wall of cope that is all over social media at present. Is Nvidia Corporation an imposter? Do rising receivables, a change in geographic revenue reporting, a large customer with a small audit firm, an attention-loving CEO, and yet-to-be proven LLM economics amount to anything nefarious? Not last time I looked, no.

When the Nvidia empire begins to crumble, as it will - all empires crumble, however indomitable they may seem at their peak - then analysts will look back with perfect hindsight and point out that this factor or that factoid was evidence of the impending decline. Your regular Monday morning quarterback routine. But until such time, what we have here is a business that continues to report exceptionally strong fundamentals and a stock that responds accordingly. We’ll take a look at that in a moment.

While we’re on the topic, the thing that is likely to topple "Emperor" CEO Jensen Huang isn’t any of the things that overexcited, underemployed wannabe year-1 investment banking analysts like to post on X as evidence of problems. It’s not DSOs. It’s power. As in, kilowatt-hours. As everyone knows, there isn’t enough power in the world to keep the plates spinning in all the planned datacenters in the works right now. Why isn’t there enough power? Is this somehow Nvidia’s doing?

It is not. There isn’t enough power because current generation large language models, or LLMs, are wildly inefficient with computing resources, which means they are wildly inefficient with power usage. This software design is a product of its environment. When interest rates are zero, people behave as if money is free, because … it is. And when there is an abundance of cheap electricity, people design software and hardware systems with minimal thought to future constraints or pricing. Humans don’t seem to be able to think beyond the current cycle of anything, which is why bear markets are always a surprise after bull markets, and why bull markets are always a surprise after bear markets.

Read that sentence again and you will see my point about how dumb humans are. But now, power is constrained and this is forcing a new generation of LLM design to rise to the surface. Already the major LLMs are adopting resource-limiting tools to this end. The LLM companies being funded at seed stage today will be laser-focused on using less power, because that is what tomorrow’s market will require.

Once LLMs need less power and less computing resources, that’s when Nvidia’s grip on the sector and the market will start to loosen. Because customers will begin to emulate Google (GOOGL) and start to use more ARM designs, more Broadcom (AVGO) ASIC blocks, more stuff that isn’t an Nvidia GPU.

We’re early in the AI cycle, just right at the beginning. The Nvidia GPU is to the datacenter of 2050 as the IBM mainframe was to the trading floor of 1985, i.e., a dinosaur. Of this I have no doubt. But none of that matters to the stock today, or tomorrow, or probably through year end. What matters to the stock right now is the price and volume pattern first and foremost, secondly the fundamentals, and thirdly the Treasury General Account.

Nvidia Financial Summary

There’s a full financial table at the end of this article, but this summary will serve our purposes here just as well.

The numbers remain stellar. It is in no way normal for a company with $187bn in TTM revenue to be growing that revenue in excess of 60% per year. Nor is it in any way normal to be turning more than half of that revenue into unlevered pre-tax free cashflow. And of course the $52bn pile of net cash on the balance sheet speaks for itself.

The thing that is unusual is that Nvidia’s valuation is always muted vs. the company’s fundamentals. At a time when plenty of hot stocks are trading at in excess of 100x TTM unlevered pretax FCF - I give you Palantir and others - you can almost always pick up NVDA stock for half that multiple or less. And yet NVDA fundamentals remain the best of the best. To what should be put that down? I think it’s continual disbelief by the market that this combination of growth and profitability can last.

Nvidia Valuation Analysis

Not at all expensive in my view. You can scream “but those multiples are crazy” if you like, but they’re not crazy in this market environment, and we don’t get to choose the market environment. We can either play by the rules of the present environment in order to win, or we can sit it out in the hope that an environment we do like comes our way. Personally I choose to change my own approach to suit whatever hand the market deals me. Seems easier to me than standing on the front lawn yelling about the pesky kids.

Nvidia Stock Chart

NVDA stock is in a tricky spot at the moment. Here’s how I see it.

Measuring from the April lows, the stock put in an initial move up and a retrace (a Wave 1 then a Wave 2, bottoming at the .618 Fibonacci retracement of that Wave 1) setting it up for the big move up in recent months. I think that move put in a Wave 3 high at around the 300% Fibonacci extension of that Wave 1 placed at the Wave 2 low. That’s a pretty good Wave 3 right there that peaked in August, if I have my analysis correct. Then a Wave 4, which broke down through the 50-day SMA, bottomed and moved up to the lower bound of where I thought NVDA would get to post earnings but in fact it got there beforehand.

That “Target if Moons On Earnings” box has been on our NVDA chart for some weeks; it shows the range between the .5 and the .786 Fibonacci extension of the Waves 1-3 placed at the Wave 4 low as drawn on this chart. Which is another way of saying “that’s the range where Wave 5s tend to top out”. No pattern recognition system is perfect but this method is pretty good in my experience - those Wave 5 topping zones work a lot more often than chance (the bar is low in technical analysis as you know). Good enough, the stock then sells off, holds over that support zone at around $175-176 and has now bounced there to be at around $195 at the time of writing (pre-market trading the day after the earnings print).

So what next? Well, unless NVDA makes new all time highs, then we’re in for more corrective action I think. Because what happens after Wave 5s top is usually a fairly material selloff, and it hasn’t happened in NVDA stock. I know there is a lot of crying in the casino right now but that’s from people all levered and amped up on high beta no-earnings speculative names.

NVDA, the S&P (SP500), the Nasdaq (COMP:IND), none of these things are very far from their all time highs. A real correction in NVDA could take the stock down to the start of that Wave 5 - around $160-163/share say - before starting a new move up if the market continues to be bullish. On the other hand if the correction is already done and the new 5-wave cycle up started on Tuesday this week - then the stock could go a lot higher.

The Treasury General Account

Want to know why stocks dumped a little in recent weeks? Less money in the system. When the Treasury General Account fills up - as it did during the federal Government shutdown, because the federal Government has fewer ways to actually spend money - that means there is less money in the banking system which means there is less money available in the brokerage system which means there is less margin capital available and/or what capital is available comes with a higher price - and all that means is that folks buy less stocks. The more money people can get their hands on and the lower the price to get it, the higher stocks go. As the post-Covid crisis ZIRP environment demonstrates.

Nvidia and all major U.S. stocks likely have a tailwind right now, and that is the disbursement of TGA funds. To quote my colleague, macro analyst Yimin Xu:

“The good news is that TGA is now starting to move lower, meaning the government is spending cash on its reopening. On Monday, TGA moved from $959 billion to $925 billion, a notable one-day spend. If continued, which it should, we could see liquidity flowing back into the markets soon.”

Nvidia Stock Rating

So how to rate this? I think at Hold.

We remain in a bull market - even those currently buried in Andrew Ross Sorkin’s 1929 and fearing the worst must see that - and the falling TGA is likely to fuel the bull further.

Nvidia’s earnings reports remain exceptionally good.

And the stock chart looks solid; even if we get a correction I think it will be down to the $160-165 level before a new move up, and there’s every chance it just keeps moving up from this present $195 level.

So I think we have to bias on the bullish side and expect higher prices in the future. Would I pile into NVDA stock at these levels? Not personally, no, but then we were banging the table in late 2022 to buy the thing (at 56x TTM cashflow in a bear market!) so my own view is that there are always better bargains to be had than stocks that are this extended. You will make your own decision of course.

The Nvidia empire will crumble. But not today. Hold.

Alex King, CEO, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 20 November 2025.

data.title: Trump's 50-Year Mortgage Idea Could Be A Big Gift To REITs
data.description:
It was recently reported that the Trump administration is working on a new 50-year mortgage proposal to make homeownership more affordable.

Bill Pulte, director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, commented that this is a "complete game-changer" for the housing industry.

It has the potential to reduce monthly average payments, increasing the affordability of homeownership for the masses.

Pulte added that this is just one proposal among many others to increase the affordability for young home buyers. Here's what he said:

We are laser focused on ensuring the American Dream for young people and that can only happen on the economic level of homebuying. A 50-year mortgage is simply a potential weapon in a wide arsenal of solutions that we are developing right now. Stay tuned!

This news has led many of you to ask me how this could potentially impact REITs if this becomes a done deal.

The answer is that it depends on the property sector, but overall, I would expect it to benefit a lot more REITs than it hurts.

Therefore, I view this as a gift to REIT investors.

Here's a step-by-step review going through individual property sectors. Those not mentioned likely won't be significantly impacted:

Single-Family Rental REITs: Positive Impact

Government-subsidized 50-year mortgages have the potential to make homeownership more affordable, increasing the demand for housing and pushing home prices to even higher levels.

This could benefit single-family rental REITs, like Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH), by increasing their portfolio net asset values. It would also potentially remove or at least mitigate the fears of a housing crash, which has hurt their market sentiment in recent years.

Moreover, the higher portfolio net asset values would also allow them to accelerate their capital recycling plans, selling homes in the private market in order to develop new communities, earning large spreads over their cost of capital.

But on the other hand, it could also reduce the demand for rentals, slowing down future rent growth. The good news is that occupancy rates are very high and rent growth is solid right now at 3-5%, so even with a dip in demand, the fundamentals should remain relatively strong.

I think that the net impact would be positive for these REITs, increasing their property values, accelerating external growth, and boosting market sentiment, at the cost of slightly lower rent growth.

Invitation Homes is today priced at one of its lowest valuations ever, trading at an estimated 40% discount to its net asset value, so the upside could be significant if you remove the fear of a housing market crash.

Self-Storage REITs: Very Positive Impact

This is likely the biggest winner of this new change.

Self-storage REITs, like Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), and CubeSmart (CUBE), suffered from weak demand in recent years because few people were moving around.

When housing is unaffordable, transaction activity slows down, and people stay put, reducing the demand for self-storage space.

But if the 50-year mortgage is now introduced, it could spark a new wave of homebuying, which would likely lead to a spike in new demand for self-storage as more people move from one home to another.

This has historically been one of the strongest demand drivers for self-storage, so the impact could be significant.

Today, self-storage REITs are priced at exceptionally low valuations due to the weak demand currently slowing down their growth. This means that the sector offers significant upside potential as demand potentially accelerates from here, in part thanks to the new 50-year mortgage.

Specialty REITs: Positive Impact

There are also a few specialty REITs that could benefit.

More demand for housing would also lead to more construction activity, which has historically resulted in more demand for lumber, increasing its prices. This then benefits timberland REITs, which harvest a portion of their trees each year.

Higher commodity prices then flow straight to the bottom line, resulting in higher cash flow and dividend payments.

Secondly, if this leads to more construction activity, it could also lead to more better use conversion projects, selling timberland to housing developers.

Rayonier (RYN) has noted that such conversion projects can increase the value of the land 10-15x:

Farmland REITs like Farmland Partners (FPI) could also benefit from such better-use conversion projects to build more housing:

Finally, the newly listed Millrose Properties (MRP) could also be a big winner since it owns primarily homesites that are waiting to be sold or developed.

It's a recent spin-off from the homebuilder, Lennar (LEN), which still owns 20% of its equity, and has option contracts to buy home sites off Millrose over time.

It's essentially the only "land banking REIT," and it could win a lot from greater housing affordability.

Retail REITs: Positive Impact

A lot of retail REITs own neighborhood strip centers in growing suburbs of Sunbelt markets. These properties are often surrounded by single-family housing communities and ample land that's zoned for additional housing.

Therefore, these retail properties are set to indirectly benefit from the growing demand for single-family houses, as it could lead to greater population density, resulting in higher tenant sales and faster long-term rent growth.

Typically, with growing demand and ample available land, the market would quickly respond by building more retail centers to meet the demand, but this seems unlikely because retail rents are still too low to justify new development projects.

According to a study by Kimco (KIM), retail rents would need to rise by 65% on average in its markets to make new development projects economically feasible.

This explains why few new retail centers are getting built today, despite the high occupancy rates and growing demand.

It also means that rent growth is likely to continue at a solid clip for many years to come in these growing markets, and this growth could accelerate even further if we get 50-year mortgages.

Apartment REITs: Negative Impact

For apartment REITs, the net impact would likely be negative.

It would weaken demand, putting pressure on occupancy rates and future rent growth. Right now, the apartment sector is already oversupplied, so weaker demand would only make things worse.

It could also hurt the sector's market sentiment, resulting in higher cap rates and lower property values.

The good news is that the supply outlook will improve significantly in 2026 and even more so in 2027, which should make up for a potentially weaker demand.

The net impact would likely still be negative, but not that significant.

Bottom Line

Overall, if the government steps in to further subsidize the housing sector, this should benefit single-family rental REITs, self-storage REITs, timberland REITs, and certain retail REITs, but it could potentially hurt apartment REITs.

As such, it should benefit many more than it hurts, leading to higher cash flows and property values in many different sectors. At High Yield Landlord, we're closely monitoring this new development and will adapt our portfolio allocation accordingly.

data.title: Comstock Holding Remains Attractively Valued, Despite Its Spectacular Rally
data.description:
Comstock Holding Companies (CHCI) has rallied 66% this year thanks to its strong business performance and its promising growth prospects. The stock has outperformed the broad market by an eye-opening margin in almost any time horizon one can examine. For instance, it has outperformed the S&P 500 this year (+66% vs. +13%) and over the last five years (+364% vs. +85%). After such striking outperformance, it is only natural that some investors may hesitate to buy Comstock Holding, thinking that the stock has become overvalued and overripe for reversion to the mean. However, this is far from true. The stock remains attractively valued and still has ample room for future growth. Therefore, investors should not dismiss the stock.

Business Overview

Comstock Holding was founded 40 years ago and operates as an asset manager, developer, and operator of mixed-use and transit-oriented properties in Washington, D.C. It offers its customers a broad range of real estate services, such as property management, construction management, development, parking management, and security.

The vast majority of the companies that focus on real estate, such as REITs, carry significant amounts of debt. However, this is not the case for Comstock Holding, which has a rock-solid balance sheet thanks to its strong business model.

The company operates with a fee-based, asset-light, debt-free business model, which drives consistent revenue growth and offers resilience during recessions. In addition, the long-term asset management contracts secure reliable revenues and visibility into future earnings.

The long-term 2022 Asset Management Agreement, which covers the most significant properties of Comstock Holding, has generated 23% average annual growth of revenue and 35% annual growth of adjusted EBITDA over the last five years. Even better, the company does not rest on its laurels and has a promising development pipeline in place.

As shown above, Comstock Holding is currently operating 91 assets and has another 4 assets under construction as well as 11 assets under development. It is thus likely to remain in growth mode in the upcoming years.

Comstock Holding enjoys positive business momentum this year. In the first nine months of this year, the company reported a 13% increase in revenue over the prior year's period. Notably, it has grown its year-to-date revenue for 27 consecutive quarters, including 25 quarters with double-digit growth. The strong performance in the first nine months of this year resulted primarily from fee-based revenue from management subsidiaries, which surged 37% over the prior year's 9-month period.

Net income decreased 15%, but the decrease was caused by some one-time operating expenses related to the ParkX subsidiary. These costs were related to the setup of a new porter/janitorial service offering. When these costs revert to normal, the top-line growth is likely to result in growth in the bottom line as well.

Balance Sheet

Comstock Holding has a pristine balance sheet. Not only does the company abstain from debt issuance, but it also has a net cash position. As per Buffett's formula (net cash = cash + receivables - total liabilities), Comstock Holding has net cash of $28 million. This amount is 22% of the market capitalization of the stock, and hence it is undoubtedly significant.

Thanks to its rock-solid balance sheet, Comstock Holding does not pay any interest expense. Instead, it has earned net interest income of $0.8 million in the last 12 months. The pristine balance sheet of the company is a testament to the strength of its business model, which does not require the issuance of debt to fund future growth. Very few companies can grow without the use of any debt. In addition, the strong financial position of Comstock Holding renders the company somewhat resilient to downturns, in contrast to other companies, which struggle to service their debt during rough periods.

Valuation

Comstock Holding is currently trading at a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 9.6x, which is lower than the earnings multiple of most of its peers. To provide a perspective, The RMR Group (RMR) and Sun Hung Kai Properties (OTCPK:SUHJY) are trading at price-to-earnings ratios of 14.8x and 15.2x, respectively, whereas AMREP (AXR) is trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.0x.

Moreover, Comstock Holding has one of the best current ratios in its peer group and a net cash position, in contrast to most of its peers, which have some debt in their balance sheets. As mentioned above, Comstock Holding has a net cash position that is equal to 22% of its current market capitalization. If the net cash position is subtracted from the stock price, then the stock is trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of only 7.5x (=9.6*0.78). To cut a long story short, despite its spectacular rally, Comstock Holding remains attractively valued.

Long-term Outperformance

Comstock Holding has outperformed the S&P 500 in almost any time horizon one can examine. To be sure, the stock has massively outperformed the benchmark over the last decade (+573% vs. +216%), over the last five years (+364% vs. +85%), and over the last 12 months (+44% vs. +12%). Very few companies can boast of outperforming the S&P 500 with such great consistency. The consistent outperformance reveals the presence of an exceptionally strong business model and high-quality management with a long-term perspective.

Risks

As Comstock Holding engages in the real estate industry, it is inevitably sensitive to the underlying conditions in the economy and in the housing market. Whenever a recession or a housing crisis shows up, the stock of Comstock Holding is likely to come under pressure. The fee-based model and the long-term agreements of the company with its customers mitigate its sensitivity to economic crises, but still the company is not immune to downturns.

The other risk factor has to do with the aforementioned elevated operating costs reported in the most recent earnings report. These costs caused a 15% decrease in net income, despite a steep increase in revenues. While management stated that a portion of these costs is non-recurring in nature, this remains to be seen in the next earnings report, in February. The 66% rally of the stock this year signals that the investing community agrees on management's view.

Finally, Comstock Holding has a market capitalization of only $129 million, and hence it is a small-cap stock. Small-cap stocks are considered riskier than large-cap stocks, as they have less firepower to endure a potential prolonged downturn due to their small size. On the bright side, the healthy balance sheet of Comstock Holding increases the resilience of the company to crises.

Final Thoughts

When most investors think about real estate stocks, they think about REITs, which often carry material amounts of debt. This is not the case for Comstock Holding, which has a net cash position thanks to its reliable, fee-based model. The company has 40 years of expertise in its business and has proved capable of outperforming the S&P 500 over the long run. Only the best-of-breed companies can boast of such an accomplishment. Even better, despite its outperformance, the stock remains attractively valued, and hence it is likely to continue rewarding investors with a long-term perspective.

data.title: Artisan Global Discovery Fund: Q3 Delivers Strong Results Amid Trimming And Exits
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Global Discovery Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The portfolio generated a modestly positive absolute return in Q3, but it trailed the MSCI All Country World Small Mid Index. The Q3 underperformance was driven by stock selection in the IT, financials and consumer discretionary sectors.

Amid the market excitement surrounding AI, our semiconductor, hardware and equipment names, such as Pure Storage (PSTG), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), were strong contributors to the portfolio's relative performance. However, our software industry holdings, including Tyler Technologies (TYL) and Atlassian (TEAM), were a significant drag. Given the rapid advancements in AI capabilities, investors have become more uncertain about which software and services companies can successfully integrate AI into their products and which will have their business models severely disrupted.

Within financials, recent market and economic conditions have been more favorable for banks and other businesses that are credit-sensitive and spread-based. However, as a quality-focused growth manager, these are businesses that just won't typically meet our investment criteria.

On the positive side, stock selection in the health care and industrials sectors was the strongest contributor to performance. Some of the earlier fears over pharmaceutical tariffs and US drug price controls appear to have moderated. Still, the success within health care, the portfolio's largest sector exposure, was driven by strong company-specific results, particularly among our biotech holdings.

Health care is still not a popular sector for investors, hindered by policy uncertainty and understandably overshadowed by investor fascination with all things AI. However, we always say that where there's innovation, you tend to find profit cycles. And plenty of innovation is still occurring within health care.

Among our top contributors were Modine Manufacturing (MOD), Pure Storage and Ascendis Pharma (ASND). Modine is a global leader in thermal management solutions. The company is experiencing strong growth, driven by increased business with existing hyperscale customers and the addition of new accounts in the hyperscale segment. Rising demand across newer geographies, particularly in the US and Southeast Asia, and Modine's expansion into advanced product lines, such as rack-level and modular data center cooling solutions, have further fueled this momentum. As a result, the company has significantly raised its revenue expectations for the year.

Pure Storage is redefining enterprise data storage by sourcing raw NAND flash memory and building custom all-flash systems, which enhances cost efficiency. The company is gaining share in core enterprise markets, and its collaboration with SK hynix for hyperscale data center environments supports its efforts to expand its addressable market. During the quarter, Pure Storage reported revenue rose 13% year over year to $861 million, driven by enterprise demand and strong performance in its subscription services and next-generation product portfolio. While cloud and hyperscaler traction are still early, core business metrics remain solid.

Ascendis is a biotech company leveraging its proprietary TransCon™ platform to develop differentiated therapies that address unmet medical needs. This technology enables sustained and predictable drug release while preserving the drug's original mechanism of action. The launch of Yorvpath®—the first and only hormone replacement therapy for adult hypoparathyroidism—has exceeded expectations, generating revenues of €103 million in Q2 2025, more than double the prior quarter, with over 3,100 patients and 1,500 US prescribers. This strong uptake reinforces our confidence in both Yorvpath® and Ascendis' broader pipeline.

Among our Q3 detractors were London Stock Exchange Group (LNSTY), PROCEPT BioRobotics (PRCT) and Wingstop (WING). London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) is a leading global provider of market infrastructure and capital markets services. We are attracted to its ability to further monetize its proprietary data, particularly through its early partnership with Microsoft (MSFT). During the quarter, competitive pressures intensified in the Workstation segment, with rivals—especially FactSet (FDS)—cutting prices aggressively. While we believe LSEG has responded well and strengthened its offering from a lower price point, these pressures have created short-term headwinds. Additionally, the emergence of advanced AI models, such as Anthropic's Claude for Financial Services, has raised questions about potential disruption to financial information services. However, we view these large language models more as new distribution channels for LSEG's proprietary data than as threats to its core business. Despite our continued confidence in the company's long-term outlook over the next 24 months, these near-term challenges have led us to trim the position.

PROCEPT BioRobotics develops robotic systems for treating benign prostatic hyperplasia and is rapidly expanding in a largely underpenetrated market, supported by strong clinical evidence, improving insurance coverage and superior outcomes. Shares fell despite reporting 55% year-over-year revenue growth and rising margins, due to the unexpected retirement of its CEO. We view the appointment of new CEO Larry Wood, a former Edwards Lifesciences (EW) executive, as a meaningful upgrade to lead the company's next phase of growth.

Wingstop, a quick-service restaurant franchisor, reported quarterly results that exceeded expectations, but shares declined due to broader weakness across the restaurant industry. We remain confident in key growth initiatives, including expanded national advertising and the rollout of smart kitchen technology. Early results showed this smart kitchen technology has reduced customer service wait times by 40% within weeks of implementation, improving operational efficiency and enhancing the customer experience at minimal cost to franchisees. We are also optimistic about the planned 2026 launch of Wingstop's loyalty program, designed to enhance customer engagement and encourage repeat business. We added to our position during the quarter.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in Teledyne Technologies (TDY) and Waystar (WAY). Teledyne provides advanced sensing, transmission and analysis technologies across niche markets, including digital imaging, instrumentation, and aerospace and defense electronics. Its aerospace and defense segment is benefiting from rising global military spending, which is driving demand for high-performance sensors, drones and space systems. Flagship products like nano drones and proprietary sensor technologies position Teledyne well to meet evolving defense needs. The company's industrial markets are also recovering, supported by trends in automation and testing. Teledyne has consistently generated strong free cash flow, and management has a proven track record of value creation through disciplined M&A. With accelerating defense demand and a rebound in industrial markets, we see potential for mid-teens EPS growth driven by both organic growth and strategic expansion.

Waystar is a leading cloud-based revenue cycle management platform that supports over 30,000 health care organizations, including 16 of the top 20 US hospitals. We initiated a position based on our view that Waystar is well positioned to benefit from the fallout of last year's cyberattack on its largest competitor, Change Healthcare. While provider switching takes time, early signs indicate this shift is underway. Additionally, we believe Waystar stands to gain from integrating AI capabilities into its software, delivering measurable ROI by automating denial management and prior authorizations, helping providers overcome staffing constraints and improve claims performance.

In addition to Wingstop, we also added to RBC Bearings (RBC) and Parsons (PSN) during the quarter. RBC Bearings is a leading provider of specialty bearings, gearing and motion control products, with over 70% of revenue derived from sole- or primary-sourced components—underscoring its critical role in customer supply chains. Its strategy of producing ahead of demand supports strong delivery performance and quality, while proprietary design software enhances product engineering. We added to our position during the quarter, as we believe the company is well positioned to benefit from the production ramp-up of next-generation aircraft, supported by long-term contracts with Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), as well as steady aftermarket demand for maintenance and repair. The company recently issued a positive outlook, supported by a strong backlog and the successful integration of its VACCO acquisition, both of which signal continued growth potential.

Parsons is a technology-driven defense, intelligence and infrastructure engineering firm recognized as a leader in cybersecurity, missile defense and critical infrastructure protection for government and commercial clients. We believe the company is well positioned to capitalize on multiple secular growth drivers—including rising global defense spending, increased North American infrastructure investment fueled by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, expanding infrastructure development in the Middle East as economies diversify beyond oil, and long-term opportunities in per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances remediation. We believe Parsons remains early in its growth journey relative to more established peers and is poised to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit organic revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, with modest margin expansion supporting a mid-teens compound annual growth rate in earnings per share.

We ended our investment campaigns in Atlassian and Bright Horizons (BFAM) during the quarter. Atlassian is a leading provider of team collaboration software. We believe the company's recent results reinforce the rationale behind our decision to exit the position. While cloud revenue growth remains healthy at 26% year-over-year, the deceleration from prior quarters suggests limited near-term acceleration in cloud migration. Ongoing concerns about Atlassian's seat-based pricing model, alongside broader uncertainty about the impact of AI on knowledge-based jobs, continue to weigh on sentiment. In our view, Atlassian must evolve from its traditional SaaS model toward a more usage-based or value-based monetization approach. However, this strategic shift lacks strong valuation support unless executed rapidly and effectively. Our evolving perspective on SaaS fundamentals over the past six months, combined with the belief that meaningful upside is unlikely until its AI investments begin delivering substantial revenue, ultimately led us to fully exit the position.

Bright Horizons is a leading provider of employer-sponsored childcare and dependent care, offering full-service centers and flexible backup care well suited to hybrid work environments. We initiated our position in December 2024, anticipating a return-to-office catalyst. With over 60% of earnings from its scalable backup care business and underutilized centers expected to benefit from rising enrollments, we saw long-term potential. However, despite early signs of recovery, enrollment growth has remained modest and uneven, particularly in younger classrooms. Continued macro uncertainty and delayed office returns have created near-term visibility challenges, leading us to exit the position.

In addition to London Stock Exchange, notable trims in the quarter included Tyler Technologies and Clearwater Analytics (CWAN). Tyler Technologies provides end-to-end information management solutions and services for local government offices. When we purchased the stock in 2020, our investment thesis focused on its potential for durable growth, driven by the resilience of its end markets and the opportunity presented by its transition to cloud-based subscriptions. As anticipated, the shift to the cloud has accelerated revenue growth and contributed to margin expansion. We trimmed our position during the quarter as the stock's valuation increasingly reflected these benefits, but we continue to hold it in the Crop.

Clearwater Analytics provides cloud-based investment accounting, compliance and risk reporting solutions for insurers, corporations and asset managers. Recently, the company shifted its strategy from focusing on organic growth to pursuing larger scale M&A, deploying significant capital and taking on leverage. This pivot was not part of our original investment thesis, so we trimmed our position. Given the high quality of the franchise, we continue to monitor it closely for evidence that the company can successfully execute this new strategy.
